_readme.what_this_is: A Foresight archetype: the durable, transferable standard for a class of website. The Verifier scores a real repo against these checkpoints. Any capable model can grade against it because the judgment lives in the rubric, not the model.

the_walls.pattern_instance: Everything here is PATTERN (transferable). Instance details are generated at runtime into the project and never written back here. This is both the design principle and the legal line: facts about good software are free to encode; specific code is not.
the_walls.strength_blindspot: Checkpoints tag domain:backbone or domain:design. Backbone ENFORCES the builder's own judgment; design SUPPLIES judgment the builder lacks.

confidence_tags._why: HONESTY MECHANIC. Each checkpoint carries a confidence field stating how well-grounded its standard is. This is the rubric refusing to dress a guess as a fact - the same discipline the tool enforces on the builder, applied to itself.
confidence_tags.established: Backed by widely-accepted engineering or documented design/accessibility specs. Thresholds defensible, not invented.
confidence_tags.reasoned: Follows sound principle but specific thresholds are judgment calls; treat numbers as tunable defaults.
confidence_tags.taste_limited: Captures the measurable FLOOR of a quality that is ultimately partly aesthetic. The rubric verifies the floor, NOT the ceiling. A model grading this catches 'clearly bad' reliably and 'genuinely great' only weakly. Bring a human eye here.

_readme.how_scoring_works: Levels 3/6/9. 3 = present but crude/risky. 6 = solid, shippable. 9 = great. Verifier reports level + concrete gap to next level. TRUST THE GAP, NOT THE NUMBER. Goal: 9 on critical, 6+ elsewhere - never infinite polish.
_readme.how_verification_works: Each verify block has reasoning (a question a model answers from the repo - the v1 spine) and assertions (mechanical checks). Assertions tagged static (AST/file inspection), test (a test must exist), or runtime (needs running build / headless browser). A checkpoint may be reasoning-only until assertions are implementable.

applies_when: The site takes money for goods/services: a catalog, a cart or equivalent, and a payment flow.

goal_definition.honest_ceiling: design checkpoints tagged taste_limited verify to ~level 7 mechanically; reaching 9 requires human design review the rubric cannot replace.

checkpoints.title: Atomic stock hold before payment
checkpoints.why: Two buyers race for the last unit; without an atomic hold placed BEFORE the payment intent, you oversell. Cheap to design in first; brutal to retrofit after payment is live.

levels.3: Stock checked then payment created as separate non-atomic steps (race window exists).
levels.6: Stock reserved atomically before payment-intent creation, with a reservation that expires on abandonment.
levels.9: Atomic reserve-then-confirm with expiry, idempotent against retries, reconciliation releasing orphaned holds; correct for both quantity stock and unique items.

verify.reasoning: Trace cart to payment. Is stock reserved atomically BEFORE the payment intent? What releases the reservation on failure/abandonment? Is there a race window between availability check and decrement?

assertions.check: A stock-reservation operation is invoked before payment-intent creation in the same transaction/flow.
Reservation has an expiry/release path.
A concurrency test fires two checkouts for the last unit; exactly one succeeds.

checkpoints.title: Idempotent payment handling
checkpoints.why: Retries, double-clicks, and webhook re-delivery cause double charges or duplicate orders without idempotency.

levels.3: No idempotency key; duplicate submissions can double-charge.
levels.6: Idempotency key on payment creation; client duplicates de-duplicated.
levels.9: Idempotency across the full path including provider webhooks (each event processed exactly once), with safe replay.

verify.reasoning: Can the same payment be created twice from a retry? Are provider webhooks processed exactly once? Is there an idempotency key on creation and webhook handling?

assertions.check: Idempotency key passed to payment provider on intent creation.
Webhook handler de-duplicates by provider event id before mutating state.

checkpoints.title: Order/payment state cannot diverge
checkpoints.why: Order status and actual payment status drifting apart (paid-no-order, order-unpaid) is a top source of silent revenue loss.

levels.3: Order marked paid optimistically on client return, before provider confirmation.
levels.6: Order transitions driven by verified provider webhooks, not client redirects.
levels.9: Explicit state machine, reconciliation job catching divergence, per-order audit trail.

verify.reasoning: Is paid set from a verified server-side signal or the client redirect? Can an order be paid without confirmation, or charged without an order? Is there reconciliation?

assertions.check: Order-paid transition triggered by a verified webhook, not a client success route.
A reconciliation path compares provider state to order state.

checkpoints.title: Ownership checks on user-scoped resources
checkpoints.why: IDOR - accessing another user's order/address by changing an id - is invisible externally and a top AI-code hole.

levels.3: Resources fetched by id with no ownership check.
levels.6: Every user-scoped read/write verifies the resource belongs to the requester.
levels.9: Centralized authorization enforced by default, with tests proving cross-account access is denied.

verify.reasoning: For endpoints fetching a resource by id (order, address, payment): does each verify ownership? Can user A reach user B's record by changing the id?

assertions.check: User-scoped fetches filter by owner id, not resource id alone.
A test attempts cross-account access and gets 403/404.

checkpoints.title: Money stored and computed safely
checkpoints.why: Floating-point money causes rounding errors that compound across totals, tax, and refunds.

levels.3: Prices as floats; arithmetic in floating point.
levels.6: Integer minor units (cents) or decimal type throughout; consistent rounding.
levels.9: Minor-unit integers, explicit currency per amount, centralized money type, rounding rules tested.

verify.reasoning: How is money represented in schema and calculations? Are floats used in price/tax/total math? Is currency explicit on each amount?

assertions.check: No float type backs price/total/tax fields.
Currency stored alongside every monetary amount.

checkpoints.title: Product/variant model fits the real goods
checkpoints.why: Conflating product with sellable unit is a foundational data-model mistake, expensive to unwind once orders reference it.

levels.3: Single product table; variants faked via duplicate products or loose fields.
levels.6: Distinct product and variant entities; stock and price on the variant.
levels.9: Variant model expresses the domain's real dimensions; order lines reference variant ids with price captured at purchase.

verify.reasoning: Does the schema separate product from sellable variant? Do stock/price live on the variant? Do order lines capture an immutable variant + price, or a mutable product?

assertions.check: A variant/sellable-unit entity is distinct from product.
Order line items capture price at purchase, not by live lookup.

checkpoints.title: Inventory truth survives failures
checkpoints.why: Added in rebuild for coverage. Reservations, cancellations, refunds, and crashes leak stock counts unless inventory is reconcilable to a source of truth.

levels.3: Stock is a single mutable number adjusted ad hoc; no record of why it changed.
levels.6: Stock changes logged as movements (sale, refund, restock, expired hold) summing to current level.
levels.9: Append-only movement ledger; current stock derivable and a job detects/repairs drift.

verify.reasoning: Is stock a bare mutable number or derived from logged movements? After a refund or expired hold, is stock corrected reliably? Can you explain why a count is what it is?

assertions.check: Stock changes recorded as discrete movements with a reason.

checkpoints.title: Tax/shipping/totals computed server-side and correctly
checkpoints.why: Added for coverage. Client-computed or mismatched totals cause charge disputes and trust loss; tax/shipping must be authoritative server-side.

levels.3: Totals/tax/shipping computed client-side or trusted from the client.
levels.6: All cost math is server-authoritative; client display is informational only.
levels.9: Server-authoritative with explicit rounding order, tax/shipping rules tested against known cases, charged amount provably equal to shown amount.

verify.reasoning: Where are tax, shipping, and totals computed? Can a tampered client change the charged total? Is the amount charged guaranteed equal to the amount displayed?

assertions.check: Final charged amount computed server-side, not read from the client request.
Tax/shipping calculations have tests with known expected outputs.

checkpoints.title: Typographic scale and readability meet documented standards
checkpoints.why: BLIND-SPOT CHECKPOINT but the FLOOR is established, not taste. Readability has real specs: body legibility, line-length, line-height, consistent modular scale. The measurable part of looks-designed.

levels.3: Ad-hoc font sizes scattered in components; body text small or cramped; no consistent scale.
levels.6: Body text >= 16px; line-height ~1.4-1.6 for body; line length ~45-75 chars; defined modular scale (1.2-1.333 ratio) for headings.
levels.9: Coherent type system applied everywhere - scale, weight, spacing reinforce hierarchy; measure and rhythm hold across breakpoints.

verify.reasoning: Is there a defined type scale or ad-hoc sizes? Is body text at least 16px with comfortable line-height? Are line lengths in the readable range on content-heavy views?

assertions.check: A central type scale is defined (tokens/theme), not per-component magic sizes.
Computed body font-size >= 16px and line-height between 1.4 and 1.7.

checkpoints.title: Color contrast and accessibility floor
checkpoints.why: BLIND-SPOT CHECKPOINT, fully ESTABLISHED - WCAG defines hard numbers, no taste required. Backbone devs routinely ship unreadable low-contrast text and untouchable controls. Verifiable to a 9.

levels.3: Text/background contrast below WCAG AA in places; interactive elements unlabeled; keyboard nav broken.
levels.6: Body text meets AA (>=4.5:1), large text >=3:1; images have alt text; forms have labels; visible focus states.
levels.9: AA throughout including UI components and states; full keyboard operability; automated a11y pass with zero critical violations.

verify.reasoning: Does text meet WCAG AA contrast (4.5:1 body, 3:1 large)? Do interactive elements have labels and visible focus? Is the checkout keyboard-operable?

assertions.check: Text/background pairs meet AA contrast ratios.
Form inputs have associated labels; images have alt attributes.
An automated accessibility scan reports zero critical violations on key views.

checkpoints.title: Consistent spacing system
checkpoints.why: BLIND-SPOT CHECKPOINT. Inconsistent spacing is the clearest tell of assembled-not-designed UI. Principle established (a spacing scale); the specific scale is a reasoned default.

levels.3: Arbitrary margins/paddings; no shared scale; uneven rhythm.
levels.6: A spacing scale (e.g. 4/8px base) defined as tokens and used consistently.
levels.9: Spacing scale applied with intent so grouping and rhythm read clearly; related grouped, unrelated separated, consistent across views.

verify.reasoning: Is spacing drawn from a defined scale or arbitrary per component? Count distinct spacing values - small intentional set or sprawl? Does spacing group related content?

assertions.check: Spacing tokens are centrally defined.
Distinct raw spacing values within a small set (default <= 10; tune per project).

checkpoints.title: Clear visual hierarchy guides the eye
checkpoints.why: BLIND-SPOT CHECKPOINT and honestly TASTE_LIMITED. The floor is checkable (one dominant CTA, type scale present). Whether hierarchy is genuinely great is partly aesthetic; a model grades it only weakly - bring a human eye to confirm the ceiling.

levels.3: Uniform weight; no clear primary action; user hunts for what matters.
levels.6: One clear primary action per view; type scale and spacing establish order; secondary actions visibly subordinate.
levels.9: [CEILING NOT MACHINE-VERIFIABLE] Deliberate hierarchy on every view so the eye lands correctly without effort - confirm with human review.

verify.reasoning: On product, cart, checkout: is there ONE obviously dominant primary action? Does the type scale create order or is everything similar weight? Would a first-time visitor know where to look without reading everything? Report the measurable floor confidently; flag the is-it-great judgment as needing human confirmation.

assertions.check: Each key view has a single visually dominant primary CTA.
A defined type scale exists (precondition for hierarchy).

checkpoints.title: Consistent design system, not ad-hoc styling
checkpoints.why: BLIND-SPOT CHECKPOINT. Scattered one-off colors/styles are the clearest tell of AI-assembled UI. Principle established; palette-size threshold is reasoned.

levels.6: Shared tokens for color, spacing, type, radius; components reuse them.
levels.9: Coherent system - small intentional palette, consistent scale, reused primitives; a new page composes from existing parts with zero new magic numbers.

verify.reasoning: Are color/spacing/type defined as tokens and reused, or hard-coded per component? Is the palette intentional and small or sprawling? Could a new page be built from existing primitives?

assertions.check: Design tokens (color/spacing/type) are centrally defined.
Distinct raw color values within a small intentional set (default <= 12; tune per project).

checkpoints.title: Checkout earns trust visually
checkpoints.why: BLIND-SPOT CHECKPOINT. The floor is checkable (costs shown before payment, designed error states). Whether it genuinely feels as safe as a major retailer is partly perception - taste_limited at the ceiling.

levels.3: Totals/fees unclear or late; no reassurance; raw error messages; feels unsafe.
levels.6: Itemized totals with shipping/tax before payment; clear error states; recognizable payment UI; obvious back/edit.
levels.9: [CEILING PARTLY PERCEPTUAL] Transparent costs at every step, reassuring microcopy, polished error/empty/loading states, flow that feels as safe as a major retailer - confirm with human review.

verify.reasoning: At payment entry: are all costs visible already with no surprises? Are error/loading/empty states designed or raw? Can the user go back and edit without losing progress? Report the floor confidently; flag the feels-trustworthy judgment for human confirmation.

assertions.check: Shipping/tax/fees displayed before the payment step.
Error, loading, and empty states are explicitly designed for checkout.

checkpoints.title: Works and looks right on real screen sizes
checkpoints.why: BLIND-SPOT CHECKPOINT, mostly ESTABLISHED. Most ecommerce traffic is mobile; broken mobile checkout silently loses the majority of sales. Overflow, tap-target size, and legibility are measurable.

levels.3: Desktop-first; mobile overflow, tiny tap targets, or broken layout.
levels.6: Responsive across phone/tablet/desktop; tap targets >= 44px; no horizontal overflow.
levels.9: Designed per breakpoint (not just shrunk); mobile checkout comfortable one-handed.

verify.reasoning: Do key flows hold at narrow widths - no overflow, readable text, tappable controls? Is the layout designed for small screens or scaled down?

assertions.check: No horizontal overflow at 360px width on key views.
Interactive targets >= 44x44px on mobile.
Layouts use responsive techniques, not fixed-pixel container widths.
